items.title: Shannon entropy H(X)
items.description: I(X; Y) is defined as a difference of entropies: H(X) - H(X | Y). You need H(X), joint entropy H(X, Y), and conditional entropy before computing mutual information.

items.title: Covariance and correlation
items.description: Pearson correlation measures linear association and is bounded in [-1, 1]. I(X; Y) detects any statistical dependence (including non-linear) and is bounded by min(H(X), H(Y)) in bits, not by linear scale.

items.title: KL divergence
items.description: I(X; Y) = D_KL(P_{X,Y} || P_X P_Y): mutual information is KL divergence between the joint distribution and the independence product of marginals. Zero MI means the KL divergence is zero (independence).

items.title: Feature selection
items.description: Filter methods rank features by I(X_i; Y) to keep inputs that reduce label uncertainty. High MI features are candidates; low MI features may be dropped before modeling.

items.title: Cross-entropy
items.description: While cross-entropy compares a model distribution to labels, mutual information compares two random variables symmetrically. Both use log ratios of probabilities rooted in Shannon's framework.

items.title: Decision trees
items.description: Split criteria based on information gain in classification trees are mutual-information-style reductions in label entropy after conditioning on a feature.
